entry.title: Introduction to SAP S/4HANA Advanced Intercompany Sales
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA Advanced Intercompany Sales is a robust solution designed to streamline and optimize the sales processes between affiliated companies within a corporate group. This functionality is particularly useful for organizations where a local sales office sells products to external customers, but the goods are delivered directly from a producing company or a central warehouse. The process ensures seamless integration between sales, procurement, delivery, and billing operations, enabling efficient intercompany transactions.

Business Process Overview

 

The Advanced Intercompany Sales process involves the following key steps:

  1. Sales Order Creation: A standard sales order is created by the selling company for an external customer. The system automatically identifies if the delivering plant belongs to a different company code, making the order relevant for intercompany processing.
  2. Intercompany Purchase Order: Upon saving the sales order, the system generates an intercompany purchase order in the selling company. This purchase order is directed to the delivering company, which is responsible for supplying the goods.
  3. Intercompany Sales Order: The delivering company receives the purchase order and creates an intercompany sales order. This order is used to manage the internal transaction between the delivering company and the selling company.
  4. Delivery and Goods Movement: The delivering company processes the outbound delivery and ships the goods directly to the external customer. The system manages the goods movements, including the transfer of goods into and out of stock in transit, ensuring accurate inventory tracking.
  5. Billing: Two types of invoices are generated:
    • Customer Invoice: The selling company bills the external customer for the goods delivered.
    • Intercompany Customer Invoice: The delivering company bills the selling company for the intercompany transaction.
  6. Invoice Verification: The intercompany supplier invoice is verified against the intercompany customer invoice to ensure accuracy and compliance.

Key Features

  • Real-Time Integration: The process is fully integrated into the SAP S/4HANA value chain monitoring framework, allowing real-time tracking and monitoring of each step.
  • Valuated Stock in Transit: The system uses valuated stock in transit to manage the transfer of goods between affiliated companies, ensuring accurate financial reporting and inventory management.
  • Automated Workflows: Many steps, such as the creation of intercompany purchase orders and sales orders, are automated, reducing manual effort and minimizing errors.

Setup Steps

To enable Advanced Intercompany Sales in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502, the following setup steps are required:

  1. Enable the Process: Activate the Advanced Intercompany Sales process (scope item 5D2) through expert configuration. This involves enabling specific pre-delivered configurations.
  2. Assign Purchasing Organization and Group: Assign a purchasing organization and purchasing group to the sales organization of the intercompany sales order.
  3. Configure Pricing: Set up pricing independently for the delivering and selling companies. This includes defining pricing procedures and condition types for internal and external customers.
  4. Define Transfer of Control Dates: Configure the internal and external transfer of control dates to manage the timing of goods movements and revenue recognition.
  5. Monitor Value Chains: Use the Monitor Value Chains app to orchestrate and monitor each step of the intercompany sales process.

Analyzing the Process

To analyze and resolve issues in the Advanced Intercompany Sales process, you can use the following tools:

  • Sales Order Fulfillment - Analyze and Resolve Issues App: This app helps identify and resolve issues that may impede the fulfillment of sales orders. It provides insights into critical stages of the process and facilitates collaboration with relevant contacts.
  • Monitor Value Chains App: This app offers a comprehensive view of the entire intercompany sales process, allowing you to track the status of sales orders, deliveries, and invoices.

Conclusion

SAP S/4HANA Advanced Intercompany Sales provides a comprehensive solution for managing complex intercompany sales processes. By automating key steps and integrating them into a unified framework, it enhances efficiency, accuracy, and visibility across the supply chain. Proper setup and configuration, along with the use of analytical tools, ensure smooth operation and timely resolution of any issues that may arise.

entry.title: Service with Advanced Execution: Process Flow 6 – Preventive or Recurring Service Processing
entry.content:
Service with Advanced Execution is a new service business process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This is based on the integration of Service with Maintenance Management.

The concept of Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution is introduced in the following blog:

Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution

Within Service with Advanced Execution, there are various process flows depending on the business scenario. These process flows are distinguished based on two aspects:

  • How the process starts, and which is the first transaction.
  • What is the trigger for the creation of the maintenance order and eventually the start of planning.

Process Flow 6:

This blog showcases the process flow wherein the initial transaction is the service contract and the maintenance plan. On scheduling the maintenance plan, both the documents are created simultaneously i.e. service order with execution order item and billable maintenance order along with the linkage between the two.

Maintenance plans enable the scheduling of recurring services at predefined intervals. These services can be part of routine maintenance on a periodic basis or triggered based on usage or performance.

For e.g. A car dealership offers preventive maintenance services for customers who have purchased vehicles from them. As part of the service agreement, the dealership provides regular doorstep maintenance every 6 months, to ensure optimal vehicle performance and longevity. A time-based maintenance plan can be set up for the car. After every 6 months, the maintenance plan triggers the creation of two orders:

  • A Service Order (the commercial object).
  • A Maintenance Order (the execution object with task list).

The service order and the maintenance order are linked with each other through document flow.

In addition to the routine maintenance, the dealership also recommends a battery replacement every 2 years. This service can be planned with a strategy-based maintenance plan.

Creation of Service Contract

A service contract can be created using ‘Manage Service Contracts’ App.
The following details could be filled while creating a service contract:

  • Sold-to-Party
  • Organizational data such as Sales Organization
  • Price information such as price agreement
  • Parties involved
  • Item details such as billing plan, price information, object list, product list etc.

Creation of Maintenance Plan

A maintenance plan can be created using ‘Add Single Plan’, ‘Add Strategy Controlled Plan’ or ‘Add Multiple Counter Plan’ Apps to create time-based or performance-based single cycle plans, strategy plans or multiple counter maintenance plans respectively.

The following details could be filled while creating a maintenance plan:

  1. Select the maintenance plan category.
  2. On the Item tab, enter a new item by specifying item description and technical object. In the planning data section enter service contract, service contract item, and order type. The service contract is mandatory.
  3. Provide a name for the maintenance plan and enter the cycle details. Optionally, enter a task list.
  4. Enter the scheduling period under maintenance plan scheduling parameters tab to specify the number of days, months, or years over which scheduling will take place.
  5. The ‘Do Not Rel. Immediately’ checkbox can be marked, if the maintenance order must not be released immediately once created.

Scheduling of Maintenance Plan

Once a maintenance plan is created, the next step is to schedule it. In the ‘Schedule Maintenance Plan' App, do the following:

  1. Enter the plan number created.
  2. When Start is clicked, the scheduling list gets populated with all scheduled calls and the dates for which they have been scheduled.
  3. Once Save is clicked, depending on the call dates, the service order and its corresponding maintenance order is generated as call objects.

The service product in the service order is copied from the service contract as the execution order item. The item category for this service product is determined from the ‘Copy Item Categories - Item Category Determination’ customizing.

Similarly, the Sold-to-Party in the service order is also taken over from the service contract.

While creating the service order and the maintenance order, the order type mapping configurations (SPRO -> Service -> Transactions -> Settings for Service Transactions -> Integration -> Plant Maintenance Integration-> Map Order Types) are not relevant. The service transaction type and plant maintenance order type are user inputs in the maintenance plan.

The operations and components in the maintenance order are derived from the task list entered in the maintenance plan.

Status lifecycle in Service with Advanced Execution

                                                      Current state of an execution order item

The state diagram illustrates the status lifecycle of one execution order item along with its corresponding maintenance order in this process flow. Each block below represents a state displaying 3 different status values. The lifecycle and execution status of an execution order item along with the status of the corresponding maintenance order.

 

Release of Execution Order Item

Once the service order and execution order item details are finalized, the lifecycle status can be set to ‘Released’. The execution status on the execution order item is ‘Execution Planning’ and the maintenance order status is 'CRTD' (Created).

Note: The execution order item can be rejected only when the execution status is ‘Execution Planning’.

The document flow between the service order and maintenance order can be viewed in Transaction History assignment block. The maintenance plan and maintenance item document flow can also be viewed here.

The details of the maintenance order connected to the execution order item can be viewed in the Maintenance Order assignment block in the item.

Release of Maintenance Order

Once the maintenance order is created from the service order, the maintenance order is in planning phase. After the planning is done, the maintenance order is set to ‘Released’ by the Maintenance Planner. Then the execution of service can be started. The maintenance order status is changed from ‘CRTD’ to ‘REL’ (Released). With this status transition, the execution status on the execution order item is set to ‘In Execution’.

Completion of Execution Order Item

After the execution of service is completed by the Maintenance Technician, the maintenance order status is set to ‘TECO’ (Technically Completed).  With this status transition, the lifecycle status of execution order item is set to ‘Completed’. The execution status on the execution order item is set to ‘Execution Done’.

Once the service order is set to ‘Completed’, the completion date is updated for the call in maintenance plan scheduling list.

Summary

The distinct feature in this process flow is the creation service order and maintenance order from maintenance plan. As we proceed with the execution of maintenance order, the lifecycle and execution status of execution order item in service order are in continuous alignment with the status of maintenance order at every step.

 

Further references

To know more about the execution order item and its attributes, please refer following blog:

Deep Dive: Execution Order Item and Billable Maintenance Order

entry.title: Staffing Facet in Project Control - Professional Services Projects App (F6869)
entry.content:
Great news! You no longer need to open another app to view the staffing details for your projects. Until now you had to use the Staffing Analysis app for these details. All this information is now available within the Project Control - Professional Services Projects app.

We've introduced information about project staffing within the app Project Control - Professional Services Projects. The Staffing facet provides comprehensive information related to resource requests. This includes work package details, work items, roles, requested and staffed efforts, worker and staffing details, delivery organization, billing control category, and work package type. Additional details such as costing data and billing element details are also available.

For example, you can highlight if staffing has been cost effective by comparing the costs that you had planned and the staffing cost of the resource.

Another new feature is that you can group the staffing for different billing elements which allows you to see who is working on which contract items. (for example, fixed price and T&M)

 

This facet also supports staffing done within external staffing solutions like SAP Project and Resource Management (6JO). If there are multiple workers staffed to a resource request, you can easily view the breakdown of the staffed efforts and the staffed resource organizations.

If you have not been using the app Project Control – Professional Services Projects, please take a look at it on your home page, on the Fiori launchpad.

Here are few additional details:

 Fiori App Ref library: https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F6869')/S34

App Help :   https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/29cf986299714386847f4d4c9bb86994/db25b660143d4ffbad8e7f4825b9d83e.html?locale=en-US

 If you have additional feedback on the app, please submit them here. https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/930

entry.title: A closer look at Event based Production accounting in UPA
entry.content:
UPA and the change in Production accounting from month end to Realtime 

Unless you have been living under a rock you must have already heard of the new Universal Parallel accounting that SAP has already released and is generally available for customers.

I have been working in the SAP Controlling area since the last two decades. The onset of S/4 HANA brought in some changes relating to cost elements, allocations Actual costing and material ledger. However, the production and costing side remained unchanged since times immemorial. I haven’t seen a change in the period end closing process (WIP, Variance and Settlement) in the last decade.

This is the most significant change to this process since a long time.

 

What’s changed:

I am not going to go over all that’s changed in UPA in this post, if you are not familiar with UPA you need to first start with the excellent blog posts by Janet and Sarah on this topic

UPA Overview by Sarah

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/universal-parallel-accounting-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/13551060

 

Inventory accounting in UPA by Janet

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/inventory-accounting-with-universal-parallel-accounting-in-sap-s-4hana-2022/ba-p/13529230

 

Event-based production accounting in SAP S/4HANA is a significant change from the traditional period-based approach, with cost postings now linked to business events rather than period-end processes. This provides real-time insights and simplifies period-end closing.

Here are the key changes:

  • Real-time insights: Cost information is up to date, allowing comparison of planned versus actual costs and variance splits.
  • Simplified period-end: Traditional period-end activities are removed.
  • Parallel accounting: Enables valuation for different accounting standards, using multiple ledgers and currencies.
  • Posting: Journal entries are created with each business transaction, replacing legacy tables.
  • WIP and Variance Calculation: Calculated immediately upon business events, rather than at period-end.
  • Reporting: New Fiori apps offer better cost and variance reviews.
  • Costing: Event-based costing sheets are necessary.
  • Results analysis key: The RSEBW key is needed for event-based posting.
  • Overhead: Material and production overheads are charged immediately.
  • Transition: Can be implemented by switching order type assignments, however period end steps may still be needed temporarily for both event-based and period-based processes.
  • Settlement: Event-based uses a posting rule, instead of the traditional settlement rule.

The scope item for event-based posting is 3F0, while the traditional approach uses BEI. Event-based is the default in SAP S/4HANA Cloud and is required for universal parallel accounting.

 

 

What’s Changed in Configuration/ Setup?

To configure event-based production accounting, several key settings need to be established within SAP S/4HANA, primarily focusing on the 3F0 scope item. These configurations ensure that costs are posted correctly based on business events rather than at period end.

 

The new IMG Node for EBPA

 

Here are the essential configuration steps,

  • Event-Based Processing Keys: Verify that the event-based processing keys, RSEBW for manufacturing orders and PCCEB for product cost collectors (or ORDMS and PCCMS for manual settlement), are available within the Event-Based WIP and Variance Posting configuration activity. These keys dictate the WIP calculation method, variance split, and valid posting receivers.

Most of the variance and WIP calculation settings are now event based and moved to one single configuration node.

  • WIP Source Accounts: Assign WIP source accounts to specify which costs should be posted and capitalized as WIP and define event-based WIP account determination rules to specify the G/L accounts to which WIP or unrealized costs are to be posted. All WIP source accounts from the Define Assignments configuration activity must be included here.
  • Event-Based Costing Sheets: Create or use a pre-delivered event-based costing sheet in the Define Costing Sheets configuration activity. Ensure the "Event-Based" checkbox is selected for the costing sheet.

 

  • Valuation Variants: Assign the event-based costing sheet to the relevant valuation variants per plant in the Define Valuation Variants per Plant configuration activity. This is crucial for overhead calculations.
    • The valuation variant must have the event-based costing sheet assigned. You can’t assign a non-event-based costing sheet.
  • Order-Type-Dependent Parameters: Assign the event-based processing key (RSEBW or ORDMS) and a planned costing variant to the relevant order types (e.g. YBM1) in the Define Order-Type-Dependent Parameters for Production Orders and Define Order-Type-Dependent Parameters for Process Orders configuration activities. For product cost collectors, assign the event-based processing key PCCEB or PCCMS in Define Cost-Accounting-Relevant Default Values for Order Types and Plants. If using RSEBW or PCCEB, the assigned planned costing variant must have an event-based costing sheet assigned to it.

            

 

  • Account Determination: Ensure that the accounts for posting variances are set up correctly under the Sourcing and Procurement area, within the Automatic Account Determination configuration activity. This includes accounts for price differences (PRD), inventory posting (BSX) and offsetting accounts for inventory posting (GBB).
  • Results Analysis Key: When creating order master data, the RA Key (results analysis key) field must be set to RSEBW for event-based production cost posting. The Event-Based Posting checkmark will then be derived automatically.
  • Settlement: With the event-based approach, settlement profiles are no longer needed. The settlement type is determined by the event-based processing key. If using RSEBW or PCCEB, settlement is triggered by business events, while ORDMS or PCCMS require manual settlement.

By completing these configurations, the system is set up to automatically post WIP, overhead, and variance in real-time, based on business events rather than waiting for period-end activities.

Event based production Postings during the month

The below table shows how WIP and variances are calculated at various stages of using Event based production accounting.

 

Original Order Status

Action to the Order

New Order Status

Event-Based Posting Results

REL (Released) or PDLV (Partially delivered)

Goods issue or activity confirmation

REL (Released) or PDLV (Partially delivered)

WIP increases.

REL (Released)

Partial goods receipt

PDLV (Partially delivered)

WIP decreases.

REL (Released) or PDLV (Partially delivered)

Final goods receipt

DLV (Delivered)

WIP is cleared and variance is settled.

Manually set Complete Technically

TECO (Technically completed)

Manually select Delivery Completed

DLV (Delivered)

TECO (Technically completed) or DLV (Delivered)

Goods issue or activity confirmation

TECO (Technically completed) or DLV (Delivered)

Variance is recalculated and resettled.

TECO (Technically completed)

Manually revoke technical completion

REL (Released)

Variance is reversed and WIP is recalculated and reposted.

DLV (Delivered)

Cancel final goods receipt

PDLV (Partially delivered)

Manually unselect Delivery Completed

 

Now, I am going to create a production order and post a goods issue to show the calculation during the month. We are going to track the progress using the apps below

  • Display Line Items - Production Accounting (F7288)
  • Work in Process - Event-Based (F3498)
  • Analyze Production Costs - Event-Based (F4059)
  • Product Cost Collector Details - Event-Based (F6965)
  • Analyze Costs by Work Center/Operation (F3331)
  • Production Cost Overview - Event-Based (F6248)
  • Display Posting Rules - Event-Based (F5228)

Step 1: Create a new Production order

Event based production costs at order creation using the app “Analyze Production costs, Event Based”

At creation, no actual costs posted yet

Step 2: Post Goods receipt and Confirmation for the Production order with minor variances in quantity

WIP Is calculated and posted right at the point of goods Issue

FI Document for WIP Posting

 

The above screenshot clearly demonstrates advantages UPA and EBPA

  • Its shown that WIP gets posted during the month in Realtime across all ledgers
  • Every ledger has a separate WIP calculation as the cost estimates by ledger are different. i.e. 0L is Legal, 2L is IFRS, 4G is group valuation.
  • WIP is calculated differently for each ledger and valuation.
  • The value is different per each ledger because each ledger has a different costing variant, valuation variant, Overhead costing sheet, Different activity prices, and material prices.
  • You can also see that 2L ledger is having a different fiscal year variant (V3) instead of K4. i.e for the first time ever in SAP we can have more than one fiscal year variant in controlling. This is only possible with UPA.
  • While 0L and 4G post in 2025, 2L posts in 11/2024 (V3 fiscal year variant)

 

How to manage errors during month end

Since we are moving from a periodic posting of WIP and variances to real-time, we need to think closely about error handling.  The below apps allow for monitoring and handling errors during the month.

 

  • Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Overhead Calculation (F5763)
  • Order Exception Monitor - Event-Based (F5133)
  • Manage Product Costing Errors (F5132)
  • Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Product Costing (F3669)
  • Schedule Product Costing Jobs (F3683)
    • Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Product Costing (Job Template)
    • Postprocess Event-Based Postings - Overhead Calculation
    • WIP Quantity Calculation for Actual Costing: Event-Based Order and PCC (only if WIP revaluation is needed in Actual Costing (33Q))

 

Errors can be monitored using the above applications.

Conclusion

Event-Based Production Accounting (EBPA) in SAP S/4HANA represents a significant paradigm shift in how companies manage their production accounting processes. This new approach offers several key advantages:

  1. Real-time insights: By posting costs as they occur, EBPA provides up-to-date financial information, enabling more timely decision-making.
  2. Simplified period-end closing: The elimination of traditional period-end activities streamlines the closing process, potentially reducing workload and improving efficiency.
  3. Enhanced parallel accounting: EBPA supports multiple ledgers and currencies, facilitating compliance with various accounting standards simultaneously.
  4. Improved cost visibility: New Fiori apps offer better tools for reviewing costs and variances, enhancing cost control and analysis capabilities.
  5. Automated overhead allocation: Material and production overheads are charged immediately, providing a more accurate picture of costs throughout the production process.

However, implementing EBPA requires careful configuration and a shift in mindset from periodic to event-driven accounting. Companies must ensure proper setup of event-based processing keys, WIP source accounts, costing sheets, and valuation variants. Additionally, they need to establish robust error handling processes to manage issues that may arise during real-time postings.

 

While the transition to EBPA may present initial challenges, the long-term benefits of more accurate, timely, and flexible production accounting make it a valuable evolution for organizations using SAP S/4HANA. As companies continue to adapt to this new approach, they are likely to discover further opportunities for optimization and improved financial management in their manufacturing processes.

 

Q&A


Feel free to ask any questions or share your experience in the comments section below. I will try to answer questions as soon as I can. Alternatively, you can reach out to me on my LinkedIn page below. Follow me there for further updates,

https://ca.linkedin.com/in/naveen-pidikiti

entry.title: Parcel, LTL, FTL Processing with Advanced Shipping & Receiving in SAP Transportation Management
entry.content:
This blog post explains how companies (like OEMs) can process their outbound freight from their distribution centers where the Warehouse and Transportation functions are well integrated allowing a possibility to automate the Transportation Planning and Warehouse execution function.

During Transportation Planning, focus is high on standard Vs. express shipping conditions agreed with the customer and in turn deciding when and what to be shipped as Parcel or LTL/FTL. As we know, each of this impacts the delivery SLAs and Costs incurred.

With Advanced Shipping and Receiving (ASR) functionality warehousing and transportation, functions together seamlessly, to achieve key benefits like:

  • Facilitate and prioritize the Pick, Pack, Stage and Load operations for Express shipments
  • Avoid a situation where pallets prematurely occupy and block staging area space waiting for the transport to arrive.

What is Advanced Shipping and Receiving (ASR) 

Let us understand this process from the perspective of an OEM selling Machines, Tools, Equipments and Spare parts.

Dealers of this OEM places orders of different kinds, some are to replenish inventory which means large number of order lines for equipments, urgent orders with very few line items for spares and some orders which are mix of equipments and spares. 

OEM wants to keep the transportation cost as minimum as possible and normally negotiates rates with Parcel Companies and LTL/FTL Carriers. Thing to note here is LTL/FTL is palletized cargo where as Parcels are just loose cartons.

Shipping conditions are enforced by the Dealers while placing the order, normally Standard Shipping or Express Shipping (in case of urgency).

Parcel has its own definition on "Shipment Size and Weight threshold" as published by different service providers like FedEx, UPS, DPD, Postal companies etc. Which also means, not every Express Shipment is a Parcel Shipment.

OEM decides the best way to ship the order lines making sure that delivery is on time as also the cost is kept minimum.

 

Shipping Condition

Qualifies for Parcel 

Shipping Possibility 1Shipping Possibility 2ASR-Type
ExpressYesParcel Warehouse Driven
ExpressNoLTLFTLTransport Driven
StandardYesLTL ParcelTransport Driven
StandardNoLTLFTLTransport Driven

Technical Implementation:

  1. As demonstrated in the "process flow" below, Sales Order information is fed into the SAP TM Freight Unit Builder (FUB) to decide what can be shipped "Parcel" and what can go LTL/FTL. Invoking a BRF+ decision table Parcel related "Shipment Size and Weight Threshold" can be checked. (FUB) spits out Parcel and Non-Parcel Freight Units
  2. Parcel Carrier determination for the Parcel Freight Units is done based on the available Parcel Rate Contracts stored in the SAP TM.
  3. Express Parcels need immediate warehouse execution, such Freight Units are so identified as Warehouse Driven ASR scenario. Functionality of Delivery initiation for Unplanned Freight Units is used to initiate the Warehouse activitiesParcel & Non Parcel Process Flow
  4. Using automated consolidation jobs Non-Express Parcels and Non-Parcel Freight Units can be consolidated into LTL/FTL shipments and follow the Transport Driven ASR process. This avoids premature warehouse execution, which could block the warehouse capacity.
  5. Non-Express Parcel Freight Units which could not be shipped under consolidated LTL load can still be pushed as Parcel Warehouse driven process.
  6. Parcel Shipping Labels can be printed from the Packing Station in the Warehouse

Such end-to-end automated process running daily has a potential to increase the efficiency of the Distribution Center.

entry.title: Warum Change Management bei einer SAP Cloud Migration wichtig ist - ein Blick in die Praxis
entry.content:
In der aktuellen Folge des EducationNewsCast Podcast diskutierte ich mit Philipp Blaesing von Siemens Healthineers und Anja Scheurer von SAP die entscheidende Rolle des Change Managements bei SAP Cloud Migrationen, insbesondere im Rahmen des RISE-Programms. Hier könnt ihr reinhören auf 🎧 SAP Podcasts - Apple - Spotify. Anbei eine Zusammenfassung als Blog. 

Das RISE-Projekt bei Siemens Healthineers: Eine globale IT-Initiative

Das RISE-Projekt bei Siemens Healthineers ist eine der größten IT-Initiativen des Unternehmens. Es umfasst die Migration von über 20 SAP-Systemen und angrenzenden Applikationen in die SAP Private Cloud.

Philipp Blaesing betont: "Innovation hat bei uns einen hohen Stellenwert bei Siemens Healthineers. Und da in Zukunft alle Innovationen in der RISE Cloud entwickelt werden, hat natürlich auch die Migration bei uns eine sehr hohe Visibility und Wichtigkeit."

Technische Migration vs. Geschäftsprozess-Transformation: Ein zweistufiger Ansatz

Ein interessanter Aspekt des Projekts ist die Trennung von technischer Migration und Geschäftsprozess-Transformation. Blaesing erklärt: "Wir trennen die technische Migration von den Business-Transformationsprojekten. [...] Erst ein stabiles Fundament gießen, wenn man mal so eine Metapher von einem Hausbau sieht und dann kann man die Räume draufbauen, wie man sie will."

Obwohl es sich primär um ein technisches Projekt handelt, unterstreicht Anja Scheurer die Bedeutung des Change Managements:

"Auch wenn RISE-Projekte auf den ersten Blick eben nur als ein technisches Projekt scheinen, betreffen technologische Veränderungen, technologische Transformationen immer die Menschen, die sie dann schlussendlich auch natürlich anwenden müssen."

Die Rolle des Change Managements in technischen Migrationsprojekten

Die Zusammenarbeit zwischen Projekt- und Change Management ist eng verzahnt. Anja Scheurer erläutert: "Grundsätzlich arbeiten wir eng auf Programmebene, sowohl aber auch eben auf der Projektebene mit den jeweiligen Verantwortlichen zusammen." Das Change Management-Team fungiert als Cross-Workstream im Programm und gibt die Methodik und den Change- und User-Kommunikationsplan auf Programmebene vor.

Die Verbindung von Change- und Projekt Management 

Change Management und Projektmanagement waren bei der SAP Cloud Migration von Siemens Healthineers eng verzahnt:

  • Integration: Change Management als Cross-Workstream im Gesamtprogramm.
  • Klare Rollen: Deutliche Aufgabenteilung zwischen PMO und Change Team.
  • Operative Einbindung: Change-Experten in Projektterminen und Cutover-Meetings.
  • Kommunikation: Während das PMO die interne Programmkommunikation vorantrieb, fokussierte sich das Change Management Team auf die Nutzer- und Nutzenkommunikation.
  • Ressourcenbereitstellung: Das Change Management Team stellte den Projektleitern Werkzeuge und Materialien zur Verfügung, um Change-Aktivitäten umzusetzen.

Diese enge Zusammenarbeit ermöglichte eine durchgängige Berücksichtigung von Change-Aspekten und schnelle Reaktionen auf Herausforderungen im Projektverlauf. 

Bewährte Methoden und Tools im Change Management

Bei den eingesetzten Methoden und Tools hat sich das Team anfangs am ADKAR-Modell orientiert, dieses aber an die spezifischen Bedürfnisse des Projekts angepasst. Bewährt haben sich klassische Stakeholder-Analysen, Engagement-Pläne und Change-Readiness-Checks. Blaesing betont auch die Wichtigkeit eines guten People-Networks und vielfältiger Kommunikationskanäle.

Learnings und Tipps für erfolgreiche SAP Migrationsprojekte

Als wichtigste Lernerfahrung nennt Philipp Blaesing die frühzeitige Einbindung des Change Managements:

".. auf jeden Fall das Change Management so früh wie möglich zu involvieren, also schon in den Planungsphasen." Anja Scheurer ergänzt: "Unterschätzt den Bedarf einfach überhaupt nicht für das Change Management und der Kommunikation in Migrationsprojekten, bindet eben die Kollegen und Kolleginnen früh ein."

Auch gaben beide konkrete Tipps für die Praxis:

  • Eine klare RACI-Matrix für Change- und Kommunikations-verantwortlichkeiten erstellen
  • Vielfältige Kommunikationskanäle nutzen (Newsletter, Webseiten, Q&A-Sessions ... )
  • Regelmäßige Feedback-Runden mit Stakeholdern durchführen
  • Change Management als offiziellen Cross-Workstream im Programm etablieren

Inspirierende Aussagen aus dem Podcast

"Wenn man das Gefühl hat, man kommuniziert zu viel, dann war es richtig."
"Change Management ist sehr viel mehr in den Mittelpunkt gerückt als vor fünf bis zehn Jahren und gewinnt immer mehr an Wichtigkeit."

Resümee

Die Erfahrungen von Siemens Healthineers zeigen, dass eine frühzeitige Einbindung, klare Strukturen und vielfältige Kommunikation entscheidend sind. 

Was ich wichtig finde ist auch folgendes: Change Management erweist sich auch bei primär technischen Projekten als wichtiger Erfolgsfaktor. Die Handlungsfelder sind vielleicht etwas anders. So gibt es beispielsweise wenig End-User Training. Aber Änderungen die Prozesse, Jobs und Aufgaben betreffen gibt es auch bei technischen Projekten. Und auch Fragen zum Mehrwert eines solchen technischen Projekts und die Besprechung und Kommunikation dieser Vorteile. Und hier ist eben Change Management sehr wichtig mit all seinen Facetten, von Stakeholder Analysen bis Kommunikation

⁉️ Habt ihr weitere Beispiele, was bei sehr technischen Projekten besonders ist rund um Change Management? Freue mich auf Kommentare hier auch. 

Shownotes

Buch: Die 5 Sprachen der Mitarbeitermotivation

Buch: Der Change Code von Dieter Lederer

Doku der Community-Session mit Anja zum SAP Change Starterkit

SAP News: Siemens Healthineers entscheidet sich für RISE with SAP um Leistung zu steigern

Aus-und Weiterbildung bei Siemens Healthineers

entry.title: Routing: Alternative Sequence & Parallel Sequence in SAP
entry.content:
When creating routing in SAP, there are 3 different types of sequences that you can create:

    • Standard Sequence

      A standard sequence is the sequence of operations that describes a production process and from which parallel and alternative sequences can branch. 

      When we create a routing, the system will automatically create a sequence, the standard sequence. The other sequence is either an alternative or a parallel sequence, but they will be connected with the standard routing. Alternative sequences are only for routings, but parallel sequences can be used for routings and rate routings.

    • Alternative Sequence

      An alternative sequence is a sequence of operations that can be used as an alternative to a number of consecutive operations from the standard sequence. This enables you to produce a product in different ways.

      Alternative sequences are used, for example, if the production process differs according to lot size, or if there is a downtime. This is also useful when there is capacity overload, and resource or machine breakdown.

      In creating a new routing sequence, you can go to routing CA01 and choose the Sequences icon.



      Choose the new entry icon to select which sequence you want to create.

      In the sequence details, decide the reference data by entering the branch operation and return operation. 

      Branch operation is from which an alternative sequence is to replace the reference sequence. This is the point in the routing where the system leaves the main sequence and enters an alternative sequence. It serves as the decision point to switch from the standard sequence to an alternative sequence.

       

      Return operation is the operation where the system exits the alternative sequence and returns to the main sequence. After the alternative sequence is completed, production resumes from the return operation, ensuring continuity in the production process.

      Meanwhile the alternative sequence fields are filled with the range of lot size of the production. For Example, the lot sizes are from 200 to 500. So, the system will automatically trigger to use the alternative sequence if the production order lot size is 200 or more until 500.

      • Parallel Sequence

        A parallel sequence enables you to process several operations at the same time and is carried out as the corresponding section in the standard sequence. These are special types of overlapping operations or if several operations are processed simultaneously.  This is the case in the process industry.

        This is useful for minimizing lead times, increasing output, and improving utilization. For example, when we handle complex products, when there is high volume production, also important if there’s no dependency between 2 operations.

        But there are considerations of using parallel sequences which will be complex in planning, monitoring, and resource availability.

        To create a parallel sequence, choose the parallel sequence when creating a new entry.
        In the sequence details, decide the reference data by entering the branch operation and return operation. For example:

        Branch operation is set for operation 0060 which is visual inspection. Then, the Return operation is set for operation 0070 which is labelling. This means all operations from 0060 to 0070 are running at the same time or processed simultaneously.

        So that’s basically the way you create a new routing sequence. This sequence will impact on the production order creation after the MRP runs are created. The difference of the sequence will determine which workstation or resource will be used in the production execution.

        Cheers,
        Dafina

entry.title: EWM Layout-Oriented Storage Control (LOSC) – A simple guide
entry.content:
This post will show you how to set up and test Layout-Oriented Storage Control (LOSC) in SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM). We'll focus on the customization and steps to follow the LOSC process. To start, let's imagine this scenario in an imaginary warehouse:



Step 1: Arrival at the Warehouse (Storage Type 9010)
Goods arrive at the warehouse dock and are unloaded into storage type 9010. They are scanned and entered the EWM system, starting their journey.

Step 2: Quality Inspection (Storage Type 0030)
Next, the goods move to storage type 0030 for quality inspection. Here, each item is checked to ensure it meets the required standards.

Step 3: Intermediate Storage (Storage Type 0035)
After passing the quality check, the goods are transferred to storage type 0035, an intermediate storage area. This is a temporary holding zone.

Step 4: Final Storage (Storage Type 0040)
Finally, the goods reach storage type 0040, the final storage area. Here, products are organized and stored until they are ready to be picked up and shipped to customers.


CUSTOMIZING STEPS

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Master Data -> Define Storage Types

Note: ensure that you have created the activity areas and storage bins for the storage types used in this process. Also, execute the bins sorting.

 

 

 

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Define Warehouse Process Type

Note: The WTP for "LOSC" was copied from 1010. Ensure the "Select HU w/o Storage Process" field is checked, as LOSC only works for handling unit warehouse tasks. If unchecked, a product warehouse task will be created, and the process won't work as intended.

 

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Define Control Indicators for Determining Warehouse Process Types

 

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Determine Warehouse Process Type

 

  • SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Warehouse Task -> Define Layout-Oriented Storage Process Control

 Note: In this scenario, we're using the same WPT for LOSC movements. For better traceability, you can create a WPT copied from 3030 to separate the LOSC steps.


TESTING
 

  • Step 1 – Goods receipt

    T-Code: MIGO

Inboud delivery created:

 

  • Step 2 – Inbound Delivery

T-Code: /SCWM/PRDI
Note: WT we’re created automatically using a PPF action but this is a subject to another post.


Note: HU we’re automatically created using packing specification.

 

  • Step 3 – Warehouse Management Monitor

T-Code: /SCWM/MON
Two warehouse tasks (WT) were created because we are using LOSC. The first warehouse task remains with status "B – Waiting":


The second warehouse task uses WPT "3030" to move the handling unit from 9010 to 0030 and has the status "Open":


Confirm the second WT:

A third warehouse task is created automatically when the second is confirmed:


The first WT has the source storage type and bin updated but remains with status "B – Waiting":


The third warehouse task is created to move the HU from 0030 to 0035:

Confirm the third WT:


The first WT has been updated and then the last move of HU is done:


Confirm the first WT:

Process done!


CONCLUSION

Using SAP EWM LOSC helps the warehouse efficiently manage the flow of goods through different storage types. This process ensures products are inspected, stored, and organized in the best way possible, leading to faster order fulfillment and higher customer satisfaction.

I try to make it simple, hope this guide helps you guys.

Best Regards,
Yuri Nogueira
SAP EWM Consultant

entry.title: Implico at the SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference 2025
entry.content:
As the energy sector navigates rapid digital transformation and sustainability challenges, Implico is at the forefront of delivering innovative solutions to optimize supply chains and enhance fuel retail operations. From April 8-10, 2025, in Rotterdam, we will join leading energy and technology experts at the SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference and showcase how cutting-edge automation and SAP solutions can empower companies to adapt and thrive in a changing market. 

We are proud to present two key sessions at this event: a workshop session on the Pre-Conference Day April 8 and a co-customer presentation at the main event on April 9, both featuring real-world applications of our solutions.

 

1. Optimizing Fuel Retail Operations with SAP Solutions 

Speakers: Jean-Marc Delbos (SAP) & Thomas Weggelaar (Implico) 

Efficient management of fuel retail networks is essential for profitability and customer satisfaction. This joint session with Jean-Marc Delbos from SAP and Thomas Weggelaar from Implico will focus on enhancing the end-to-end processes of fuel retail operations using SAP’s Retail Fuel Network Operations (SAP S/4HANA RFNO) solution and Implico’s automation technologies. 

 Key Takeaways: 

  • Unlocking operational efficiencies through SAP S/4HANA RFNO and automated data flows. 
  • Real-time fuel inventory monitoring and replenishment. 
  • Reducing operational overhead and carbon emissions through process automation. 

 

2. Automatic Trip Planning for Bulk Logistics 

Speaker: Adrian Mägerle (Migrol/Switzerland) & André Bente (Implico) 

(IDM) by Implico is a robust solution designed to enhance capabilities within SAP S4SCSD. Fuel distribution logistics is a complex puzzle that requires precision and efficiency. In this session, Adrian Mägerle from Migrol AG, one of Switzerland’s leading energy services providers, will explore together with André Bente from Implico how automatic trip planning optimizes the logistics in supply chain and secondary distribution by leveraging SAP-integrated tools and Implico’s Integrated Dispatch Management (IDM) capabilities. 

Key Takeaways: 

  • Reducing manual planning efforts  
  • Boosting efficiency and sustainability 
  • Insights from a prototype of the new Implico SAPlement AddTCO 

 

Enhancing Sustainability through Digitalization and the Clean Core Approach 

As an SAP SOLEX Partner, Implico’s approach to sustainability focuses on combining digitalization, automation, and optimized resource allocation with SAP’s Clean Core approach, which ensures that custom business processes are decoupled from the digital core of SAP systems. This strategy allows for easier upgrades, continuous innovation, and long-term system stability. 

By leveraging clean, structured data and the Clean Core methodology, Implico enables: 

  • Efficient transport routes that reduce fuel consumption and emissions. 
  • Real-time energy usage monitoring and sustainability tracking. 
  • Smarter asset utilization to minimize waste and maximize operational efficiency. 

These solutions are designed to meet both current needs and the challenges of a greener future, including the integration of LNG, hydrogen, and other renewable fuels into existing networks. 

 

Join Us in Rotterdam for Insightful Discussions and Live Demos 

We invite attendees to visit our booth for hands-on demos of our solutions and to attend our presentations to learn how we’re transforming supply chains in the energy sector. Whether you’re planning an S/4HANA migration or seeking ways to optimize fuel retail operations, Implico is here to help. 

 

Our Product Portfolio: Driving Efficiency Across the Supply Chain 

Implico’s comprehensive portfolio empowers businesses to streamline processes, reduce costs, and improve customer satisfaction.  

Learn more about our core offerings: 

  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION *** (Secondary Distribution Management) - Optimizing last-mile fuel delivery through route planning, dispatching, and real-time monitoring. 
  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION *** (Retail Fuel Network Operations) - Enhancing visibility into fuel station inventory, pricing, and replenishment needs. 
  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION *** - Automating route planning and dispatch to reduce downtime and maximize resource efficiency. 
  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION ***- Simplifying and automating processes in bulk liquid handling, from loading to transport. 
  • *** REMOVED BY MODERATION ***- Streamlining cylinder management, automating order processing, and optimizing distribution for maximum efficiency. 

 

Let’s shape the future of energy together! 

Schedule a meeting with our team:  

Visit *** REMOVED BY MODERATION *** to contact us or book a session during the event. 

 

We look forward to seeing you in Rotterdam from April 8-10, 2025!

entry.title: From User Exits to RAP: Adopting a Clean Core Approach in S/4HANA
entry.content:
From User Exits to RAP: Adopting a Clean Core Approach in S/4HANA

Welcome to our SAP blog! In this blog we’re diving into the world of Clean Core in S/4HANA and how you can achieve it using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). If you’re looking to future-proof your SAP system while extending its functionalities seamlessly, you’re in the right place. Let’s explore how RAP can help you maintain a cloud-ready, upgrade-safe system without compromising on flexibility.

 

Why Clean Core Matters

In today’s fast-paced digital landscape, businesses need agile and scalable systems. Clean Core is all about keeping your SAP system lean, efficient, and ready for the cloud. It ensures that your customizations don’t interfere with SAP’s standard processes, making upgrades smoother and reducing technical debt.

But here’s the challenge: How do you extend standard functionalities without breaking the core? Traditional methods like User Exits and BAdIs have served us well, but they’re not always cloud-compliant. Enter RAP a modern, event-driven approach to building and extending applications in S/4HANA starting from 2022.

 

Real-World Insights: The Shift to RAP

Through numerous customer engagements, my colleague Balaji @Balaji_Guptha  and I have seen firsthand the challenges of implementing Clean Core. Whether it’s a Brownfield migration or a Greenfield implementation, the goal remains the same: extend without breaking.

While traditional enhancements like User Exits are familiar, they often lead to upgrade headaches. RAP, on the other hand, offers a fresh perspective. It allows you to extend standard processes without modifying the core, ensuring compatibility with future upgrades.

 

The Power of Event-Driven Architecture

At the heart of RAP lies Event-Driven Architecture (EDA). This approach enables systems to react to changes in real-time, making it ideal for modern business processes. With RAP, you can leverage business events to trigger actions, integrate systems, and automate workflows—all while keeping your core clean.

Key Use Cases for RAP Events:

  • Real-Time Notifications: Automatically notify external systems about critical changes, like a new Sales Order or a status update.
  • Seamless System Integration: Enable third-party applications to react to business events without constant polling or synchronous API calls.

RAP Business Event Consumption

SAP provides two approaches to consuming RAP business events:

  1. Remote Event Consumption: Events consumed by external systems (e.g., SAP Event Mesh).
  2. Local Event Consumption: Events handled within the same RAP-based application without external dependencies.

This blog focuses on Local Event Consumption, which enhances system responsiveness and automation within an S/4HANA system.

Local Event Consumption: A Game-Changer

One of the most powerful features of RAP is Local Event Consumption. This allows you to handle events within the same RAP-based application, eliminating the need for external middleware. It’s perfect for scenarios where immediate follow-up actions are required.

How It Works:

  1. Event is Triggered: A change occurs in a RAP business object (e.g., Sales Order creation).
  2. Local Event Handling: An event handler class within the same application processes the event.
  3. Business Logic Execution: The handler class executes predefined logic, such as updating related entities or triggering validations.

Identifying the Standard RAP BO for Business Events

Before implementing local event consumption, identifying the correct RAP Business Object (BO) Root Entity is essential. Follow these steps:

  • Navigate to SAP Business Accelerator Hub https://api.sap.com/

 

  • Select your SAP product (e.g., S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition)

 

  • Locate On-Stack Extensibility → Business Object Interface

 

  • Search for the relevant Business Object, e.g., Sales Order

     

     

  • Once the Business Object (BO) Interface Name is identified, the next step is to navigate to Eclipse ADT (ABAP Development Tools) to determine the Event Binding and Root View Entity Name required for implementing local event consumption in RAP.

  • In Eclipse open the behavior definition for the Business Object interface I_SALESORDERTP.

     

  • In the ABAP repository tree navigate to Business Service Folder to get the Event binding name for the Business Object Interface I_SALESORDERTP.

     

  • Select the required business event, in our case it is Sales Order Created event.

    In the event binding we can find the standard RAP business object with events, R_SALESORDERTP.

     

  • Next navigate to the behavior definition of R_SALESORDERTP business object to check the events enabled for the RAP BO.

     

Identifying Business Events

SAP Provides a standardized way to explore and utilize business events through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This platform allows developers and architects to identify events relevant to their business processes and integrate them into an event-driven architecture.

To find the required business event, follow these steps:

  1. Navigate to SAP Business Accelerator Hub 
  2. Select the relevant SAP product (e.g., SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition) based on the system you are working with.
  3. Once the product is selected, navigate to the "Events" tab. This section provides a list of all available events that the selected SAP solution can trigger. Search for the business process or required event in the search bar. In this example we have considered Sales Order Events
  4. Select the event and click on it to view the detailed information.

Use Case: Automating Delivery Block in Sales Orders

Let’s look at a real-world scenario where RAP’s Local Event Consumption shines.

Business Requirement: When a Sales Order is created, the system should automatically apply a delivery block if certain conditions are met. These conditions are based on predefined rules stored in a custom table.

Traditional Approach: In the past, this would be implemented using a User Exit (MV45AFZZ). However, this approach is not cloud-compliant and can lead to upgrade challenges.

Clean Core Approach with RAP: Using RAP’s Local Event Consumption, we can achieve the same functionality without modifying the core. Here’s how:

Creating an Event Handler Class
  1. Define a global class that listens for events from the Sales Order business object.
  2. Use the keyword FOR EVENTS OF to specify the behavior definition.
Inherit from the Event Handler Superclass
  1. In the local class, inherit from CL_ABAP_BEHAVIOR_EVENT_HANDLER to access event-handling capabilities.
Define Event Handling Methods
  1. Create methods to handle specific events, such as SalesOrder.Created.
  2. Category of the custom class created will be ‘Event Handler for Entity Events’.
Implement Business Logic
  1. Logic to enable the delivery block at sales order header level.
  2. In this implementation, the Enablement of the Delivery Block in the sales order is achieved using Entity Manipulation Language (EML) instead of traditional BAPIs. 
  3. Refer to the SAP Help Portal at the following link: https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-rap/entity-manipulation-language-eml . This resource provides the syntax and detailed explanation for EML.

By implementing this, we successfully automated the application of a delivery block in a cloud-compliant manner, ensuring minimal technical debt and future-proof extensibility. This approach not only aligns with SAP’s Clean Core principles but also enhances system agility and maintainability.

 

Why This Matters

By adopting RAP’s Local Event Consumption, you’re not just solving a business problem—you’re future-proofing your SAP system. This approach ensures that your customizations are cloud-compliant, upgrade-safe, and scalable.

 

Final Thoughts

RAP is more than just a programming model—it’s a mindset shift. It encourages us to rethink how we extend SAP systems, moving away from traditional methods and embracing modern, event-driven architectures. Whether you’re dealing with Sales Orders, Purchase Orders, or any other business process, RAP provides the tools you need to keep your core clean and your business agile. So, what are you waiting for? Start exploring RAP today and unlock the full potential of your S/4HANA system!

Stay tuned for more insights on Clean Core strategies in S/4HANA!

Feel free to ask for any clarifications or share your suggestions!

Regards,

Ravdeep Singh

entry.title: AIF Automatic Reprocessing – Technical deep dive
entry.content:
Application interface framework (AIF) AIF Documentation  is an framework which is widely used in S/4 HANA, Central finance for rationalization of different kind of interfaces with universal monitoring and error handling capabilities, underlying interface technology e.g. IDOC, XML etc remains abstract to the end user (Technical / Business).

With growing use-cases of AIF in large enterprise setting, there comes different challenges and one of them is understanding of automatic reprocessing capabilities.

In this blog, we would deep dive into details of technical know-how of automatic reprocessing.

Use case – We have set up XML/Structured persistence interface in AIF and based on a specific message number, AIF should do the automatic processing in certain interval for certain times.

First Tcode which is used in this process is /n/AIF/REP_AC_DEF where reprocessing action is defined. SAP has delivered standard function module ‘/AIF/RESTART_MSG’ for this purpose which is sufficient in almost all the cases however if you have a custom requirement then custom FM based on template FM ‘/AIF/TEMPL_RESTART_AUTO_REPR’ can be created and assigned in this Tcode.

Assigned FM would be used during reprocessing job, it will pick the properties/action/logic from original interface configuration for which GUID is being reprocessed.

Assigned runtime config Id(Tcode - /n/AIF/PERS_CGR) determines how the reprocessing should occur, e.g. packet size, server group etc.

The automatic reprocessing is triggered by specific error messages for a registered AIF interface.

So the user has to register the AIF Interface and the corresponding error messages by message class and number for automatic reprocessing in the transaction /n/AIF/REP_AC_ASGN.

 

  1. Assign the reprocessing action which was defined in previous transaction.
  2. Max repr. Counter - Define a maximum number of reprocessing attempts that can be executed for a failed data message.
  3. Minimum wait time (Seconds / Minutes /  Hours) - Define a minimum waiting time that must pass before the application can trigger a reprocessing attempt. 
    e.g. If a vendor is blocked and it takes around 6 hours for master data team to unblock the vendor then Minimum waiting time can be defined as 6 hours so that reprocessing can be attempted after vendor is unblocked.
  4.  Maximum wait time (Seconds / Minutes /  Hours) - Define a maximum waiting time before which             application must trigger a reprocessing attempt.
    e.g. if you are supposed to provide a acknowledgment of original AIF message with in 10 hours then maximum waiting time could be 10 hours so that all reprocessing attempts can be made to get the latest status based on the error.
  5. Intermediate status (In Process or Error) the failed data message displays while it's waiting to be reprocessed.

Conclusion –

  1. First Reprocessing iteration should start only after “Min time” + original AIF message creation time has elapsed but No later than “Max time” + original AIF message creation time
  2. Next retry attempt of reprocessing job would occur after previous iteration completion time + “Max time” , if same errors still exists

Below FM / class method are called during original AIF message processing if the AIF error message meets the reprocessing criteria and it schedules the job based on defined reprocessing config.

 

 

Once above is executed, a job is created in ‘Scheduled’ status and can be checked in SM37. Planned start date for these jobs are defined based on Min and Max time defined in AIF reprocessing config.

Job name - /AIF/Namespace_RuntimeConfigID*

Reprocessing data is captured in table /AIF/REP_MSG_CNT which provides valuable information like how many retry attempts are made , timestamp etc.

Working example – Lets check how this config works with a working example-

We have set the config as below where maximum retries are 2, Minimum waiting time is 1 Minute and Maximum waiting time is 10 Mis.

Chain of events-

  1. Original AIF message was created on 15:38 which would schedule reprocessing job.
  2. First reprocessing job was released with a scheduled start time of 15:48 , the original message was created on 15:38 however ‘Max time’ was defined as 10 mins so reprocessing jobs has been scheduled for original + 10 mins.
  3. Second reprocessing was released with a scheduled start time of 15:58 since first iteration was completed on 15:48 so first iteration + 10 Mins.

AIF Message job log with timings.

While AIF message is waiting to be reprocessed, the status becomes ‘In Progress’ and sub status would be ‘Automatic reprocessing’ which can be checked in corresponding index table.

 

Tips –

  1. Reprocessing Configuration performed for defining actions and assigning message is not transportable and should be done in each client separately.
  2. If there is an existing job scheduled between the minimum waiting time and the maximum waiting time, the reprocessing message is assigned to this job insteda of creating a new job.
  3. Runtime Configuration Group should be active and use Run Scheduled.

entry.title: SAP just launched the Business Suite - what does this mean for Finance?
entry.content:
Feb 13, 2025 is “Business Unleashed Day” for the SAP Community, with announcements on SAP Business Suite and SAP Business Data Cloud - and how AI, data, and applications are combined like never before to help you unleash your full potential and make you unstoppable.

Whether your company has been running an SAP ERP solution for many years, or whether you’re just at the beginning of the evaluation of a Cloud ERP solution, you might be wondering what impact these announcements – especially the announcements of the SAP Business Suite - will have on your landscape planning.

Evolution from monolithic to composable

Just as technology has completely changed since ERP was first defined for 1970s mainframes, SAP’s approach to ERP has evolved to keep up you keep us with the needs of your business:

  • Modular capabilities that can be adopted quickly as new needs arise
  • Always up-to-date software-as-a-service solutions that give you non-disruptive access to innovations
  • Clear interoperability and data semantics so that you will be in the best position to realize the benefits of AI

The 2025 Gartner® ERP Primer for 2025 states that “in Gartner’s 2023 ERP, Procurement, HCM and Finance Apps Survey of those manager level and above who are responsible for ERP decision making, 71% reported their organizations were either in process or have plans to adopt a flexible and agile ERP architecture within two years.”

So, chances are, you’re already thinking about the importance of having the flexibility to choose business technology more flexibly to support your growth initiatives.

 

 

Gartner: ERP Primer for 2025, by Denis Torii, Dixie John. GARTNER is a registered trademark and service mark of Gartner, Inc. and/or its affiliates in the U.S. and internationally and is used herein with permission. All rights reserved.

 

The SAP Business Suite is SAP’s answer to Composable ERP

You know SAP as a leader in Cloud ERP for years, but the “ERP and Finance” focus of finance teams may overlook the additional, incremental value SAP can provide with our broader family of solutions – solutions that address business needs and can be adopted incrementally, like Fieldglass, Ariba, SuccessFactors, Concur, and CX.

Since the interoperability and data semantics among the applications is curated by SAP, the insights that can be achieved with SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Business AI are limitless.

 

RISE with SAP is a transformation journey aimed at helping SAP ERP customers transition to the SAP Business Suite - so they can stay ahead of the competition by continuously innovating – across their whole enterprise landscape – not just in “ERP”.

What this means is that Finance teams should be thinking of the value that SAP solutions can create in your IT landscape. For example, adopting updated, world-class procurement processes have benefits specifically for the CFO in terms of cash visibility, risk mitigation and more. You can read a great blog on the topic here.

The SAP Business Suite approach encourages companies to think beyond their administrative ERP footprint: how can we adopt the best solutions to solve all types of business problems – for the Chief Revenue Officer, or for the Chief Human Resources Officer?

Cloud software and software-as-a-service promised that business teams would be able to pick-and-choose the services that meet their business requirements. However, often IT does have to be involved to manage integrations and ensure data interoperability. The SAP Business Suite aims to make that modularity - and business user choice - promise a reality.  

And modularity is not just part of the North Star architecture at the line of business level. SAP Business Suite is a reminder that within the line of business solutions, there are software-as-a-service solutions that can be adopted to solve specific business problems – even if your current core architecture might be more monolithic than you’d like.

Composability within Financial Management

Even within the areas that are traditionally considered to be “ERP”, SAP is offering more flexibility and modularity than ever before. Based on your business needs, these SaaS solutions – with their out-of-the-box best practices and constant access to innovation - are available as extensions to SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud:

  • SAP Green Ledger - to align your carbon and financial accounting by integrating carbon emissions with your business processes, complementing SAP’s portfolio of sustainability solutions, including SAP Sustainability Control Tower.
  • SAP Advanced Financial Closing – for orchestrating financial closing activities across ERP systems
  • SAP Account Substantiation and Automation by BlackLine – to automate manual closing tasks and reconciliations
  • SAP Digital Banking – to automate connections to any financial service provider (banks, SWIFT, payment service providers) in a quick and easy way for more control
  • SAP Cloud for Credit Integration – to integrate and manage external credit rating information for completely touchless, responsive credit decisionmaking
  • SAP Trading Platform Integration – to connect to leading trading platform providers for inbound and outbound straight-through-processing of trades 
  • SAP Risk and Assurance Management - to automate compliance checks and manage remediations efficiently

These solutions – that solve specific business problems- are integrated with our standard financial management processes in Cloud ERP.

So, if you’re part of the 70% of ERP leaders thinking about flexibility in ERP, you should consider what the SAP Business Suite can do for your organization. Making the most of the SAP Business Suite will help you

  • Manage uncertainty through effective steering and agile planning
  • Navigate transformation supported by efficient processes to balance growth and profitability
  • Enable compliance and sustainability in an ever-evolving regulatory and risk environment

Learn more about Finance solutions here.

entry.title: Production Planning Master Data: Bill of Material
entry.content:
What is Bill of Material in Production Planning?

In a simple word, Bill of Material (BOM) is a structured list of all components (raw materials, semi-finished goods, finished goods) required to manufacture a finished product. It defines the relationship between a product and its components in Production Planning and Material Requirements Planning (MRP). BOM are use in several manufacturing types such as discrete manufacturing, process manufacturing, and repetitive manufacturing in SAP system.


Example of Bill of Material table


Example of Bill of Material structure

Bill of material is used to determine the required quantity of components (MRP), defines the production process and operations for the materials in the BOM, production order who used BOM to consume components in manufacturing, and also used to calculate the material costing. SAP will calculate the price for each material in the bill of material to be included in the cost of goods manufactured.

SAP supports various BOM types depending on the production and business requirements:

  • Material BOM – Used in production to define product structures.
  • Equipment BOM – Used in Plant Maintenance (PM) for maintenance planning.
  • Functional Location BOM – Used for maintenance at a specific location.
  • Sales Order BOM – Specific to customer requirements in Make-to-Order (MTO) scenarios.
  • Variant BOM – Supports configurable materials with different variations.
  • Multi-Level BOM – A hierarchical BOM where components also have their own sub-components.


Now let’s talk about how to create a BOM.

You can go to CS01 and insert the material, plant, and BOM usage. 
Then, go to the BOM header.

Choose the BOM status ‘1’ if you want to set the BOM active.

Then insert the components for this material. Also choose the item category and quantity.

So that's basically how you create a BOM. Feel free to discuss more if you have any other insights or questions!

A lifetime learner,
Dafina

entry.title: RAP On-Stack Developer Extensibility
entry.content:
Problem statement:
How to develop custom extensions for standard ABAP RAP based business objects in S/4HANA Public, Private Cloud, BTP ABAP Environment and On-Premise Platforms?

Solution:
RAP Developer Extensibility is the solution. The topic gives an overview of RAP developer extensibility architecture, extension development artifacts such as BO interfaces, all available extensibility features and how to develop RAP custom extensions such as data model extension, behavior extensions and node extensions for standard ABAP RAP based applications.

I am on of the presenter for this topic at ABAP Conference and int the below youtube recording. This topic covers contented related to extensibility architecture overview, extensibility use cases,  RAP BO Interfaces Concept, How Procurement Project Standard RAP based application is extended with custom extensions and some useful links which can be explored related to RAP Developer Extensibility. 

ABAP Conference recording:  Deep Dive into RAP Developer Extensibility

Slides: https://abapconf.org/abapconfeurope2024/w1w2.html

Check for topic "Deep Dive into RAP Developer Extensibility" where you can find the link to download slides in pdf document.

Use cases covered:

  1. Data Model Extensions 
    • Adding Custom fields to standard CDS views
  2. Behavior and Field related behavior extensions 
    • Adding Dynamic feature control for custom field based on standard field.
    • Adding a new Custom Action
    • Adding Validation for standard field.

  3. Node Extensions
    • Adding a new custom node for a standard RAP BO.
  4. Service Definition Extension

Note:  Demo shown in youtube recording for procurement project application is implemented without using wizards. That means all required extension objects are created manually in ADT.

In ABAP Cloud in the latest version you can also use below ABAP Repository object wizard for generating extension fields to the selected  RAP Business Object.

Below TechEd git code sample exercises give more information about developer extensibility generation using above wizard 
RAP630 - Use ABAP Cloud for developer extensibility where Developer Extensibility is used.
Exercise 3 - Extend the CDS data model using the field extension wizard

You can find out all released RAP BO Interfaces in SAP Business Accelerator Hub in the On stack Extensibility section.

entry.title: Streamline Data Migration from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA: Selective Data Transition with Time-Slice
entry.content:
Enhanced Selective Data Transition Capabilities with Time-Slice

On top of the possibility to leave behind outdated company code data, the selective data transition scenario in SAP BTC now includes further capabilities to reduce the data scope. These enhancements allow for even more precise data selection, ensuring that only the most critical and relevant data is migrated. This not only optimizes the data transition process but also supports compliance with data retention policies and regulatory requirements.

What is Time-Slice?

Time-Slice is a powerful feature within SAP Business Transformation Center's Selective Data Transition scenario that allows organizations to selectively transition data from the legacy SAP ECC system based on a specific key date. This means you can choose to migrate only the most relevant and recent data, leaving behind outdated or unnecessary information of fiscal years from the past. By doing so, you not only reduce the volume of data being transferred but also enhance the efficiency and speed of your SAP S/4HANA data migration projects.

SAP Business Transformation Center allows organizations to reduce the amount of data moved from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA based on the dimensions company codes & fiscal years to reduce the data footprint as shown below:

 

Read the documentation about the time-slice solution pattern.

Watch this video, to get the full picture of the time-slice solution pattern!

 

Benefits of Using Time-Slice

Reduce your Data Footprint by transitioning only the necessary data, you can significantly decrease the amount of data stored and managed, leading to cost savings and improved system performance.

 

Enhance your Data Quality by focusing on recent and relevant data ensures that your migrated data is accurate and up-to-date, reducing the risk of errors and inconsistencies. This can be verified using integrated Data Transition Validation (SAP DTV) tooling. 

Migrate Faster with a smaller data set to transfer, the migration process becomes quicker and more efficient, minimizing downtime and disruption to your business operations.

Improve the Compliance with selective data transition adhering to data retention policies and regulatory requirements by ensuring that only the required data is retained and migrated.

 

How Time-Slice and Selective Data Transition Work

The Time-Slice feature in SAP BTC is designed to be user-friendly and easily customizable. Here’s a step-by-step overview of how it works:

1. System Analysis: Conduct a thorough analysis of your existing data to identify the relevant time periods and data sets for migration using SAP Readiness Check for Usage and Data Profiling.

In Manage Digital Blueprints app, in the solution pattern section for “Time-Slice”, data insights into the data usage across the different years are conducted. This provides an idea of the system age and data used in the previous years.

2. Define Scope: With the analysis file attached, use the Manage Digital Blueprints app to create a Digital Blueprint and to specify the time periods and company codes, that need to be transitioned. Furthermore, attach a system scan to include also additional tables such as custom tables into the migration scope). In the solution pattern section for time-slice in the Manage Digital Blueprints app, the user has the possibility to define the fiscal year where the data should be taken from:

 

3. Create a Transformation Model: Set up the Transformation Model based on your scoping exercise covered in the Digital Blueprint to create the technical foundation of your migration. The Transformation Model delivers pre-defined standard content for the selective data transition including filtering for company codes and time-slice.

4. Create a Transformation Model Version, based on your Transformation Model which is a frozen “ready-to-execute” technical foundation for your data migration.

5. Create Migration Cycles: Set up migration cycles based on the Transformation Model Version that contains the defined scope, which is ensuring a smooth selective data transition for only the selected company codes and fiscal years.

6. Set up Data Validation (optionally): Use the integrated data transition validation (DTV) tool to compare the data of the source system with the data in the target system after load.

7. Run the Data Migration: Walk through the execution steps in the Manage Cycles app to execute Data Transformation: Perform the Pre-validation, Preparation, Transformation Execution and the Post-Processing.

 

Real-World Success Stories

Many organizations have already noticed the benefits of using Time-Slice in SAP BTC. For instance, a leading manufacturing company can reduce its data footprint by 77% and cut down migration time by 50%, all while maintaining data integrity and compliance. Due to the intuitive and standardized guided data migration process, overall data migration planning, implementation and execution effort is very low compared to similar projects in the past.

Conclusion

The Time-Slice feature in SAP Business Transformation Center is a game-changer for businesses looking to optimize their data migration processes. By selectively transitioning data, you can reduce your data footprint, enhance data quality, and achieve faster, more efficient migrations. Embrace the power of Time-Slice and take your data management strategy to the next level with SAP Business Transformation Center's Selective Data Transition scenario.

 

 

Further information

E2E Documentation

E2E Product video demonstrating SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA lean selective data transition (without time-slice feature)

Time-Slice Feature Video

Other blogs: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-business-transformation-center-lean-selective-data-transition/ba-p/13931763

entry.title: Frequent Error Messages during Asset Accounting data migration to S/4HANA
entry.content:
Frequent Error Messages during Asset Accounting data migration to S/4HANA

During the conversion of an ERP solution to SAP S/4HANA, several frequent errors may occur in Asset Accounting (FI-AA) data migration. Below is a summary of the most common errors and their resolutions:

Error FINS_RECON761:

  • Possible Causes:
    • If the error comes up in phase R25 and the difference is very small for the field NAFAP in tables FAAV_ANLC_ORI and FAAV_ANLC, it is due to rounding and can be ignored.
